A basic analysis: Visiting by Extraterrestrials
================
5/21/2021

==============================================================================

### **About**

**title:** *A basic analysis: Visiting by Extraterrestrials*

**author:** Evrim Bilgen

### **aims&benefits**

The project aims at providing an accessible introduction to various R
methods. That also taught me the understanding of interactive web
applications. Even if I wanted more control of the application
functionality understanding the key concepts was challenging. I came up
with this idea regarding its proper and well-adjusted data form. And of
course, being a fan of X-Files is another fact. I tried to build some
histogram, graph, map visualization for our analyses.

### **materials&method**

-   Writing own functions in R
-   Advanced data processing with dplyr,dtplyr,tidyr
-   Automation of scripts and reports (RMarkdown)

### **packages**

You can see our major packages below

    * library(tidyverse)
    * library(tidytext)
    * library(shiny)
    * library(dplyr)
    * library(RSQLite)
    * library(shinythemes)
    * library(shinydashboard)
    * library(shinyWidgets)
    * library(shinyalert)
    * library(DT)
    * library(plotly)
    * library(maps)
    * library(leaflet)
    * library(readr)
    * library(ggplot2)
    * library(RColorBrewer)
    * library(markdown)
    * library(knitr)

-   shiny.rstudio.com
-   medium.com
-   nielsvandervelden.com
-   kaggle.com

-   towardsdatascience.com

-   bookdown.org

<center>

![](https://blogs.sps.ed.ac.uk/neuropolitics/files/2016/10/I-want-to-believe-X-Files-UFO.jpg)

## Let’s meet our data

Our data is provided by the National UFO Reporting Center including USA
states.

``` r
library(dplyr)
```

    ## 
    ## Attaching package: 'dplyr'

    ## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
    ## 
    ##     filter, lag

    ## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
    ## 
    ##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

``` r
ET <- read.csv("./Data/ETdata.csv", header=TRUE)

glimpse(ET)
```

    ## Rows: 63,561
    ## Columns: 8
    ## $ date_sighted <chr> "1949-10-10", "1956-10-10", "1960-10-10", "1961-10-10", "…
    ## $ latitude     <dbl> 29.88306, 28.97833, 21.41806, 36.59500, 41.11750, 33.5861…
    ## $ longitude    <dbl> -97.94111, -96.64583, -157.80361, -82.18889, -73.40833, -…
    ## $ city         <chr> "San Marcos", "Edna", "Kaneohe", "Bristol", "Norwalk", "P…
    ## $ state        <chr> "TX", "TX", "HI", "TN", "CT", "AL", "FL", "CA", "NC", "NY…
    ## $ shape        <chr> "cylinder", "circle", "light", "sphere", "disk", "disk", …
    ## $ duration_sec <dbl> 2700, 20, 900, 300, 1200, 180, 120, 300, 180, 1800, 180, …
    ## $ comments     <chr> "This event took place in early fall around 1949-50. It o…

As you can see that our data have 8 columns. And below, you can see the
top 10 rows; describing in which state was visited by UFO and which
cities were they and what they were like exactly.

    ##   date_sighted latitude  longitude       city state    shape duration_sec
    ## 1   1949-10-10 29.88306  -97.94111 San Marcos    TX cylinder         2700
    ## 2   1956-10-10 28.97833  -96.64583       Edna    TX   circle           20
    ## 3   1960-10-10 21.41806 -157.80361    Kaneohe    HI    light          900
    ## 4   1961-10-10 36.59500  -82.18889    Bristol    TN   sphere          300
    ## 5   1965-10-10 41.11750  -73.40833    Norwalk    CT     disk         1200
    ## 6   1966-10-10 33.58611  -86.28611  Pell City    AL     disk          180
    ##                                                                                                                                                     comments
    ## 1                    This event took place in early fall around 1949-50. It occurred after a Boy Scout meeting in the Baptist Church. The Baptist Church sit
    ## 2                 My older brother and twin sister were leaving the only Edna theater at about 9 PM&#44...we had our bikes and I took a different route home
    ## 3 AS a Marine 1st Lt. flying an FJ4B fighter/attack aircraft on a solo night exercise&#44 I was at 50&#44000&#39 in a &quot;clean&quot; aircraft (no ordinan
    ## 4                 My father is now 89 my brother 52 the girl with us now 51 myself 49 and the other fellow which worked with my father if he&#39s still livi
    ## 5                                          A bright orange color changing to reddish color disk/saucer was observed hovering above power transmission lines.
    ## 6                                    Strobe Lighted disk shape object observed close&#44 at low speeds&#44 and low altitude in Oct 1966 in Pell City Alabama

Our first analysis tab includes first plot. And also there are two
inputs, one button on the left side.

We built this structure using navbarpage including 3 tabpanels and their
inputs

<center>

![Screenshot 1](images/im1.png)

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Our first plot is representing the number of visits as the type of shape
depending on the state and date information.

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The second plot is presenting cities that UFO visited mostly in the
chosen state.(above)

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

And the last tab is about general statistics information depending on
the state and date information and some calculation about the shapes.

Here below, you can see basic calculations such as min, max, the average
duration of the seeing shapes.

``` r
ET %>%
      filter(
        state == 'AL',
        date_sighted >= '2010-01-01',
        date_sighted <= '2020-01-01'
      ) %>%
      group_by(shape) %>%
      summarize(
        nb_sighted = n(),
        min_duration_min = min(duration_sec) / 60,
        max_duration_min = max(duration_sec) / 60,
        avg_duration_min = mean(duration_sec) / 60,
        Total_shape = length(shape)
      )
```

    ## # A tibble: 19 × 6
    ##    shape     nb_sighted min_duration_min max_duration_min avg_duration_min
    ##    <chr>          <int>            <dbl>            <dbl>            <dbl>
    ##  1 changing           4           4                120              34.5  
    ##  2 chevron            2           0.25               0.75            0.5  
    ##  3 cigar              3           0.0833             5               2.36 
    ##  4 circle            25           0.0333            75               9.52 
    ##  5 cross              3          30                120              60    
    ##  6 cylinder           7           0.5               15               6.64 
    ##  7 diamond            7           0.0333             8               3.58 
    ##  8 disk               4           0.167             25              13.3  
    ##  9 egg                4           0.167              3               0.875
    ## 10 fireball          21           0.0667            30               3.49 
    ## 11 flash              8           0.0333            45               8.20 
    ## 12 formation          8           0.167             30               5.75 
    ## 13 light             52           0.0167            60               9.67 
    ## 14 other              9           0.25             120              17.4  
    ## 15 oval               8           4                 45              16    
    ## 16 sphere            18           0.0333           240              22.0  
    ## 17 teardrop           3          15               2880             972.   
    ## 18 triangle          15           0.0833            10               3.43 
    ## 19 unknown           13           0.167             60               8.36 
    ## # … with 1 more variable: Total_shape <int>

Now, we see our second tab panel. Mapping with leaflet was a great way
to show our data interactively.

For controlling the result; You can select a shape that listing in the
input and also pick a date for more specific results

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

In addition to these, you can also determine duration by using the
slider input on the right top of the map. This input explains the shape
that you chose how long it had been seen.

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

We also preferred to use **add legend** property in our leaflet code
chunk for separated the shapes using different colors.

      color <- colorFactor(topo.colors(15), Data$shape)

      output$map <- leaflet::renderLeaflet({
      byduration() %>%
      leaflet() %>% 
      addTiles() %>%
      setView( -98.58, 39.82, zoom = 5) %>% 
      addTiles() %>% 
      addCircleMarkers(popup = ~ comments, stroke=TRUE, fillOpacity=0.5,
                       color=~color(shape)
          ) %>%
          addLegend(
            "bottomleft", # Legend position
            pal=color, # color palette
            values=~shape, # legend values
            opacity = 1,
           title="Types of shape")
           

When you click on those shapes by mouse icon, you can see the popup
comment including some information about the exact shape.

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Now we are heading on our last tab panel which is actually including a
**R Markdown** file. (also this HTML presentation ) This RMarkdown file
aims mainly at the aspect of the shiny app besides some instructions
about how you can use this app efficiently. And of course, you can also
find information about the project, code and us.

<center>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### **Thanks for your attention**

### **The truth is out there**
